learning_outcomes.description: speak and write in an informed way about the Bible and its interpretation
appreciate the complexities of biblical language and culture

assessments.due_date:
3/08/2026 - 19/10/2026

Tutorials start in week 2 for 10 weeks excluding public holidays.
assessments.task_description:
There will be ten tutorials over the course of the semester starting in week 2.

Please note that active participation is required. Tutorial participation requires genuine preparation, which includes completing the set readings and attending the lecture. Participation marks are not given simply for attendance (see the criteria below).

Students are expected to attend 8/10 tutorials to be eligible for full participation marks (2.5 x8 = 20 marks total).

Absences: Students who are absent for more than 2 tutorials should contact the course coordinator prior to the tutorial and must complete the written response (diary entry) found in the library resources page for this course and submit it by email within 1 week.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Topic: Students can choose any topic they wish to write on. They can feel free to consult with the tutor and lecturers.

Referencing Style: Students are strongly encouraged to use Chicago style for references and bibliographies: http://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/tools_citationguide/citation-guide-1.html

Word Limits: 2,000 words. Within ±10% of the set length (word limit) is acceptable. Word count does not include footnotes or bibliography, however, discursive footnotes are included in the word count. A word count that is outside these 10% will receive a proportionate penalty and will be graded against the grading criteria. 

AI: This assessment task evaluates the student's abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of artificial intelligence (AI). Students are advised that the use of AI technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
End of Semester Exam Period

7/11/2026 - 21/11/2026

This exam is scheduled centrally and will take place during the exam period at the end of semester. All students are required to attend in person.
assessments.task_description: This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction

This lecture offers an introduction to the course, its assessment, and prompts students to think about the Bible as an historical and contemporary artefact of continuing relevance.
learning_activities.topic_description: Introduction This lecture offers an introduction to the course, its assessment, and prompts students to think about the Bible as an historical and contemporary artefact of continuing relevance.

learning_activities.topic:
How was the Bible Written I: From Scrolls to King James

This lecture introduces the complex history of how the Bible came to be written and compiled together as canon. It traces the Bible's origins from ancient Hebrew scriptures to Christian canons, including key translations, such as the Latin Vulgate and King James Version.
learning_activities.topic_title: How was the Bible Written I: From Scrolls to King James
learning_activities.topic_description: How was the Bible Written I: From Scrolls to King James This lecture introduces the complex history of how the Bible came to be written and compiled together as canon. It traces the Bible's origins from ancient Hebrew scriptures to Christian canons, including key translations, such as the Latin Vulgate and King James Version.

learning_activities.topic:
How was the Bible Written II: Making the Bible

This lecture looks at how different Christian canons emerged, and why various Christian traditions include different books in their bibles. It explores historical impacts of the Septuagint, the Protestant Reformation, and scholarly theories like the Documentary Hypothesis.
learning_activities.topic_title: How was the Bible Written II: Making the Bible
learning_activities.topic_description: How was the Bible Written II: Making the Bible This lecture looks at how different Christian canons emerged, and why various Christian traditions include different books in their bibles. It explores historical impacts of the Septuagint, the Protestant Reformation, and scholarly theories like the Documentary Hypothesis.

learning_activities.topic:
How was the Bible Written III: Canon from Conflict

This lecture examines how various conflicts, including between Jews and Romans, as well as Christian responses to heresies like Marcionism, Gnosticism, and Montanism, helped to shape the biblical canons we have today.
learning_activities.topic_title: How was the Bible Written III: Canon from Conflict
learning_activities.topic_description: How was the Bible Written III: Canon from Conflict This lecture examines how various conflicts, including between Jews and Romans, as well as Christian responses to heresies like Marcionism, Gnosticism, and Montanism, helped to shape the biblical canons we have today.

learning_activities.topic:
The Bible, Reason, Tradition and Experience in the Christian tradition

This lecture explores the Bible and its role in reason, tradition and experience, drawing on the text: God: A new Biography (London: I.B. Tauris, 2018)
learning_activities.topic_title: The Bible, Reason, Tradition and Experience in the Christian tradition
learning_activities.topic_description: The Bible, Reason, Tradition and Experience in the Christian tradition This lecture explores the Bible and its role in reason, tradition and experience, drawing on the text: God: A new Biography (London: I.B. Tauris, 2018)

learning_activities.topic:
The Bible and Popular Culture

The Bible continues as a source to fuel imagination and story-telling, from Hollywood films to politicians who rely on ideas from the Bible to secure a moral population through returning to Biblical roots.
learning_activities.topic_description: The Bible and Popular Culture The Bible continues as a source to fuel imagination and story-telling, from Hollywood films to politicians who rely on ideas from the Bible to secure a moral population through returning to Biblical roots.

learning_activities.topic:
Empire and the Bible

This lecture begins a block of three that introduce historical contexts and indigenous perspectives to think through the relationships of Christianity, the Bible, and colonisation. The first spans the 15th c. Papal Bulls, which divided the world between Spanish and Portuguese for the purposes of conquest, and the 1823 American legal case Johnson vs McIntosh, which utilised the papal bulls and other documents to determine that indigenous Americans had no right to sell the lands they occupied. The legal framework developed during this period is often known as 'The Doctrine of Discovery', and was formative for the Mabo case in Australia. The lecture includes a viewing of the 2015 documentary 'The Doctrine of Discovery: Unmasking the Domination Code', by the American indigenous scholar and activist Steven Newcomb, the co-founder of the Indigenous Law Institute. The set readings interrogate the ways in which the Bible was used to justify colonisation practices.
learning_activities.topic_description: Empire and the Bible This lecture begins a block of three that introduce historical contexts and indigenous perspectives to think through the relationships of Christianity, the Bible, and colonisation. The first spans the 15th c. Papal Bulls, which divided the world between Spanish and Portuguese for the purposes of conquest, and the 1823 American legal case Johnson vs McIntosh, which utilised the papal bulls and other documents to determine that indigenous Americans had no right to sell the lands they occupied. The legal framework developed during this period is often known as 'The Doctrine of Discovery', and was formative for the Mabo case in Australia. The lecture includes a viewing of the 2015 documentary 'The Doctrine of Discovery: Unmasking the Domination Code', by the American indigenous scholar and activist Steven Newcomb, the co-founder of the Indigenous Law Institute. The set readings interrogate the ways in which the Bible was used to justify colonisation practices.

learning_activities.topic:
Indian Christianity and the Bible

This lecture explores the histories and interactions of the Syriac liturgy Christians of the Malabar coast (dating to around the 2nd c. CE, perhaps earlier), the Portuguese and Catholic missionaries (especially the Jesuits), the merging and splitting of these two communities, the arrival of Protestant colonisers and (eventually) Protestant missions that led to Bible translations into Asian languages and the emergence of Christian conversion movements that articulated Dalit liberation narratives.
learning_activities.topic_description: Indian Christianity and the Bible This lecture explores the histories and interactions of the Syriac liturgy Christians of the Malabar coast (dating to around the 2nd c. CE, perhaps earlier), the Portuguese and Catholic missionaries (especially the Jesuits), the merging and splitting of these two communities, the arrival of Protestant colonisers and (eventually) Protestant missions that led to Bible translations into Asian languages and the emergence of Christian conversion movements that articulated Dalit liberation narratives.

learning_activities.topic:
Indigenous Australia and the Bible

This lecture focuses on Australia and, specifically, Queensland. It is framed initially within the contexts of colonisation, missionary activities, the displacement (and the concomitant cultural dislocation) of peoples onto missions and government reserves. It looks at Bible translation movements within these contexts, and at indigenous peoples’ resistance, adaptation, and agency in navigating colonial contexts.  Students are introduced to three case studies of indigenous Christianities that are derived from research conducted for the Queensland Atlas of Religion.
learning_activities.topic_description: Indigenous Australia and the Bible This lecture focuses on Australia and, specifically, Queensland. It is framed initially within the contexts of colonisation, missionary activities, the displacement (and the concomitant cultural dislocation) of peoples onto missions and government reserves. It looks at Bible translation movements within these contexts, and at indigenous peoples’ resistance, adaptation, and agency in navigating colonial contexts.  Students are introduced to three case studies of indigenous Christianities that are derived from research conducted for the Queensland Atlas of Religion .

learning_activities.topic:
The Bible and Liberation: Queer and Black Readings

This lecture explores alternative readings of the Bible from historically marginalised populations, focussing on Queer theology and Black liberation theology.
learning_activities.topic_title: The Bible and Liberation: Queer and Black Readings
learning_activities.topic_description: The Bible and Liberation: Queer and Black Readings This lecture explores alternative readings of the Bible from historically marginalised populations, focussing on Queer theology and Black liberation theology.

learning_activities.topic:
The End? Revelation, the Bible, and Climate Justice

The Bible has served as a window into the past as well as into the future. This lecture will explore how the Bible resources environmentalist movements, and at times, climate change denial.
learning_activities.topic_title: The End? Revelation, the Bible, and Climate Justice
learning_activities.topic_description: The End? Revelation, the Bible, and Climate Justice The Bible has served as a window into the past as well as into the future. This lecture will explore how the Bible resources environmentalist movements, and at times, climate change denial.

learning_activities.topic:
Course Summary

This lecture provides a summary of the course and provides opportunities for discussion.
learning_activities.topic_description: Course Summary This lecture provides a summary of the course and provides opportunities for discussion.
